perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall remain in full force and effect until revoked by any party hereto in a signed writing provided to each other party hereto, and then only with respect to such revoking party.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall automatically terminate 30 days after the initial filing, regardless of any written revocation notices.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a fixed termination date (30 days) which contradicts the standard practice and legal understanding that joint filing agreements remain effective until explicitly revoked by a party, as long as the filing obligations exist.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 13 and Section 16
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13 and 16 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 mandates ongoing reporting requirements for certain shareholders. Joint filing agreements facilitate compliance with these ongoing obligations. The modified text's automatic termination after 30 days would prevent continued compliance with these sections of the Exchange Act, contradicting the purpose of the initial agreement and potentially violating the Act if reporting obligations extend beyond that timeframe.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed 30-day termination clause, directly conflicting with the original agreement's provision for termination only upon signed revocation by a party. This inconsistency undermines the ongoing reporting obligations mandated by the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The Law Explanation highlights that Sections 13 and 16 require continuous reporting, and the 30-day termination would impede compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes multiple references to the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically sections 13 (15 U.S.C. 78m) and 16 (15 U.S.C. 78p), indicating its relevance and applicability to the agreement. The changed text's automatic termination provision creates a clear conflict, as it does not allow for continued compliance beyond the 30-day period, thus directly contradicting the legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), and the rules and regulations thereunder, each party hereto hereby agrees to the joint filing, on behalf of each of them, of any filing required by such party under Section 13 or Section 16 of the Exchange Act or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto) with the Securities and Exchange Commission (and, if such security is registered on a national securities exchange, also with the exchange), and further agrees to the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto agrees to the joint filing of initial statements only, and disclaims any responsibility for amendments or updated filings required under Section 13 or Section 16 of the Exchange Act.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration limits the joint filing agreement to initial filings only, expressly disclaiming responsibility for subsequent amendments or updates. This contradicts the continuous reporting requirements mandated by the Securities Exchange Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 13(d) and 16(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: Sections 13(d) and 16(a) of the Securities Exchange Act require ongoing and updated disclosures of beneficial ownership. By disclaiming responsibility for amendments, the altered agreement fails to meet the legal requirements for continuous and accurate reporting, potentially leading to violations of securities law. The legal requirement is for continuous compliance, not just the initial filing.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 , as amended, 29 U.S.C. 1001 et seq. (“ ERISA ”) that is subject to the provisions of ERISA , or any such plan that is not subject to ERISA that is maintained primarily for the benefit of the employees of a state or local government or instrumentality, or an endowment fund; (G) A parent holding company or control person, provided the aggregate amount held directly by the parent or control person, and directly and indirectly by their subsidiaries or affiliates that are not persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J), does not exceed one percent of the securities of the subject class; (H) A savings association as defined in Section 3(b) of the Federal Deposit Insurance Act ( 12 U.S.C. 1813 ); (I) A church plan that is excluded from the definition of an investment company under section 3(c)(14) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-3 ); (J) A non-U.S. institution that is the functional equivalent of any of the institutions listed in § 240.13d-1 (b)(1)(ii)(A) through (I), so long as the non-U.S. institution is subject to a regulatory scheme that is substantially comparable to the regulatory scheme applicable to the equivalent U.S. institution; and (K) A group, provided that all the members are persons specified in § 240.13d-1(b)(1)(ii)(A) through (J). (iii) Such person has promptly notified any other person (or group within the meaning of section 13(d)(3) of the Act) on whose beha
perturbation.justification: The altered text contradicts the Securities Exchange Act by disclaiming responsibility for amendments or updated filings required under Section 13 or Section 16. The original text agrees to the joint filing of any filing required by each party under Section 13 or Section 16, including any amendment. The changed text limits this to initial statements only. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, addresses the filing of schedules 13D and 13G, stating that any person acquiring beneficial ownership of more than five percent of a class of equity security shall file a statement with the Commission within five business days after the date of acquisition, suggesting continuous obligation, and not just an initial filing. The law explanation states that sections 13(d) and 16(a) require ongoing and updated disclosures, and the alteration fails to meet the legal requirements for continuous and accurate reporting.

perturbation.original_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, each party hereto, being duly authorized, has caused this agreement to be executed and effective as of the date set forth below. Date: April 7, 2020
perturbation.changed_text: IN WITNESS WHEREOF, each party hereto, being duly authorized, has caused this agreement to be executed and effective retroactively to January 1, 2019. Date: April 7, 2020
perturbation.explanation: The modification attempts to apply the agreement retroactively. While parties can agree on terms with retroactive effects, it can create issues with prior filings and compliance, potentially conflicting with past legal obligations under securities law, especially if previous filings were not consistent with what the retroactive agreement would imply.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) requires beneficial owners to file disclosures within 10 days of acquiring more than 5% of a voting class of equity securities. Retroactively applying a joint filing agreement could create inconsistencies with previously filed (or unfiled) disclosures, potentially violating the timely filing requirements under 15 U.S.C. § 78m(d) and SEC rules implementing it if the parties did not comply at that time.

perturbation.justification: The changed text attempts to apply the contract retroactively to January 1, 2019, which contradicts securities law. The Law Explanation states that Section 13(d) requires beneficial owners to file disclosures within 10 days of acquiring more than 5% of a voting class of equity securities. By retroactively applying the agreement, it could create inconsistencies with previously filed (or unfiled) disclosures. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this by stating that retroactive application can create issues with prior filings and compliance, potentially conflicting with past legal obligations under securities law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not valid and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation are sufficient to support the conclusion that the retroactive application creates a legal contradiction.
